# T01: Taught meshc and mesh-lsp to discover scoped installed package roots from mesh.toml leaves and added scoped package regressions that prove the natural cache layout builds and analyzes cleanly.

**Taught meshc and mesh-lsp to discover scoped installed package roots from mesh.toml leaves and added scoped package regressions that prove the natural cache layout builds and analyzes cleanly.**

## What Happened

Added manifest-driven installed-package root discovery to compiler and LSP analysis so both walk `.mesh/packages` recursively until they reach the first descendant containing `mesh.toml`, then resolve modules relative to that leaf. This preserves the scoped on-disk cache layout while naturally skipping package-root `main.mpl`. Switched `meshc` and `mesh-lsp` package scans to use those roots, added direct compiler and LSP regressions for scoped and flat layouts, added a new `e2e_m034_s01` consumer build/run proof, and recorded the repo-local gotcha in `.gsd/KNOWLEDGE.md`. During verification I corrected the test fixtures to export `message` publicly and added `tempfile` to `mesh-lsp` dev-dependencies so the new temp-project LSP tests compile.

## Verification

Passed `cargo test -p meshc --test e2e_m034_s01 -- --nocapture`, `cargo test -p mesh-lsp scoped_installed_package -- --nocapture`, and `cargo test -p meshc test_build_project_discovers_scoped_installed_package_modules -- --nocapture`. These cover the scoped consumer build/run path, direct compiler package-root drift, and LSP analysis of scoped plus flat installed-package layouts. Slice-level verifier/script and docs-contract checks remain owned by T02/T03 and were not expected to pass yet.

## Verification Evidence

| # | Command | Exit Code | Verdict | Duration |
|---|---------|-----------|---------|----------|
| 1 | `cargo test -p meshc --test e2e_m034_s01 -- --nocapture` | 0 | ✅ pass | 15900ms |
| 2 | `cargo test -p mesh-lsp scoped_installed_package -- --nocapture` | 0 | ✅ pass | 7000ms |
| 3 | `cargo test -p meshc test_build_project_discovers_scoped_installed_package_modules -- --nocapture` | 0 | ✅ pass | 20000ms |


## Deviations

Added a direct `meshc` unit regression in `compiler/meshc/src/discovery.rs` so package-root drift fails earlier than the end-to-end import proof. Added `tempfile` to `compiler/mesh-lsp/Cargo.toml` dev-dependencies to support temp-project analysis fixtures.

## Known Issues

None.

## Files Created/Modified

- `compiler/meshc/src/discovery.rs`
- `compiler/mesh-lsp/src/analysis.rs`
- `compiler/meshc/tests/e2e_m034_s01.rs`
- `compiler/mesh-lsp/Cargo.toml`
- `.gsd/KNOWLEDGE.md`
tasks.description:
Close the hard blocker from research: real registry installs already extract scoped packages to `.mesh/packages/<owner>/<package>@<version>`, but both `meshc` and `mesh-lsp` currently stop one directory too early and therefore treat the owner directory as the package root. Preserve the scoped on-disk layout, teach both analyzers to recurse to leaf package roots containing `mesh.toml`, and pin the contract with named regressions that prove a consumer can import a module from a scoped installed package without manual flattening.

## Load Profile

- **Shared resources**: repeated filesystem walks under `.mesh/packages` during builds and editor analysis.
- **Per-operation cost**: recursive directory traversal plus parse/typecheck of each discovered package root.
- **10x breakpoint**: deeply nested or version-heavy package caches would hurt build/LSP latency first, so discovery must stay deterministic and skip non-package directories cheaply.

## Negative Tests

- **Malformed inputs**: owner directories with no `mesh.toml`, hidden directories/files, and package trees that contain only `main.mpl`.
- **Error paths**: nested non-package directories should be skipped instead of panicking or compiling package-root `main.mpl` as a normal module.
- **Boundary conditions**: both `.mesh/packages/<owner>/<package>@<version>` and flat `.mesh/packages/<package>@<version>` layouts resolve the same import/module naming rules.

## Steps

1. Extract or add a shared package-root discovery rule in `compiler/meshc/src/discovery.rs` that finds leaf package directories containing `mesh.toml` anywhere under `.mesh/packages`, while still ignoring hidden paths and package-root `main.mpl`.
2. Mirror the same scoped-package discovery semantics in `compiler/mesh-lsp/src/analysis.rs` so editor diagnostics/hover/go-to-definition stay aligned with `meshc build`.
3. Add `compiler/meshc/tests/e2e_m034_s01.rs` coverage that lays out a temp consumer plus scoped installed package tree and proves the consumer builds without manual filesystem moves.
4. Add or extend `compiler/mesh-lsp/src/analysis.rs` unit tests so nested scoped package roots analyze cleanly and do not regress back to the owner-directory bug.

## Must-Haves

- [ ] Scoped installed packages build from their natural nested owner/package cache path.
- [ ] `meshc` and `mesh-lsp` share the same package-root expectation for scoped installs.
- [ ] Named regressions fail specifically on discovery drift instead of surfacing later as a vague `module not found` release failure.
tasks.verify:
`cargo test -p meshc --test e2e_m034_s01 scoped_installed_package_builds -- --nocapture`
`cargo test -p mesh-lsp scoped_installed_package -- --nocapture`
tasks.observability_impact:
- Signals added/changed: named scoped-package build and analysis regressions.
- How a future agent inspects this: rerun the `scoped_installed_package` test filters in `compiler/meshc/tests/e2e_m034_s01.rs` and `compiler/mesh-lsp/src/analysis.rs`.
- Failure state exposed: whether drift came from package-root discovery, module naming, or editor/runtime divergence.

tasks.title: Add the canonical real-registry publish/install verifier and proof fixtures
tasks.description:
Once scoped installs compile locally, create the single repo-local proof surface that later CI work can call unchanged. The verifier must use one owner-scoped proof package name plus a unique version per run, isolate credentials under `.tmp/m034-s01/home`, publish to the real registry with `meshpkg --json`, and then recheck API, download, lockfile, consumer build, duplicate publish rejection, and public discoverability instead of trusting artifact builds or homepage counts.

## Failure Modes

| Dependency | On error | On timeout | On malformed response |
|------------|----------|-----------|----------------------|
| Real registry API / R2 | Fail the active phase, persist status/body/logs under `.tmp/m034-s01/verify/`, and stop without retrying the same version. | Fail fast with the package/version coordinate recorded for operator review. | Treat any non-JSON or missing field as proof failure; do not infer success from partial output. |
| Packages website SSR | Only check it after publish/install truth passes; persist the exact URL/body snapshot on failure. | Allow one bounded wait/retry, then fail the visibility phase. | Treat missing package/version text as a visibility failure, not as a soft warning. |
| Local `meshpkg` / `meshc` binaries | Stop immediately and keep stdout/stderr in the phase log. | N/A | Reject non-JSON `meshpkg --json` output and stop the proof. |

## Load Profile

- **Shared resources**: immutable registry versions, download counters, real network calls, and the temporary credential/home directories under `.tmp/m034-s01/`.
- **Per-operation cost**: one real publish, multiple metadata/search/detail fetches, one tarball download/hash, one install, one consumer build, and one duplicate-publish check.
- **10x breakpoint**: version collisions and flaky network calls fail first, so the verifier must generate unique versions and keep phase logs for reruns instead of retrying blindly.

## Negative Tests

- **Malformed inputs**: missing `MESH_PUBLISH_OWNER`, missing `MESH_PUBLISH_TOKEN`, missing/invalid version override, or templates that fail to render a quoted scoped dependency key.
- **Error paths**: duplicate publish must return 409, missing metadata/search/detail fields must fail the proof, and any SHA mismatch between publish metadata, downloaded tarball, and `mesh.lock` must stop the script.
- **Boundary conditions**: exact package detail URL, search endpoint visibility, and a consumer that installs from `mesh.toml` using a quoted scoped dependency key.

## Steps

1. Add durable proof-package and consumer fixture templates under `scripts/fixtures/` so the verifier creates reproducible temp workspaces instead of hand-writing files inline.
2. Implement `scripts/verify-m034-s01.sh` with phase helpers patterned after the existing M033 verifier style: isolated HOME/credentials, unique per-run version generation, JSON publish/install parsing, direct registry HTTP checks, tarball SHA validation, lockfile assertions, consumer build, and duplicate publish rejection.
3. Verify public visibility using the exact package detail/search surfaces rather than homepage package counts, and store every phase log/artifact under `.tmp/m034-s01/verify/` for later debugging.
4. Keep the verifier small and CI-ready: explicit env contract, deterministic temp paths, no secret echo, and no dependency on unreleased installer artifacts or workflow YAML.

## Must-Haves

- [ ] `scripts/verify-m034-s01.sh` is the authoritative publish→metadata→download→install→lockfile→build proof surface for this slice.
- [ ] The verifier uses quoted scoped dependency keys in the consumer manifest and proves the installed dependency actually compiles.
- [ ] Duplicate publish rejection, exact package detail/search visibility, and SHA/lockfile truth are all checked in the same run.
- [ ] Every failure leaves phase-specific logs under `.tmp/m034-s01/verify/` without printing secrets.
tasks.verify:
`bash -n scripts/verify-m034-s01.sh`
`bash scripts/verify-m034-s01.sh`
tasks.observability_impact:
- Signals added/changed: phase-scoped verifier logs, saved JSON/API payloads, downloaded tarball/hash artifacts, and captured `mesh.lock`/build outputs.
- How a future agent inspects this: start with `.tmp/m034-s01/verify/`, then rerun `bash scripts/verify-m034-s01.sh`.
- Failure state exposed: the exact publish/install/visibility phase, endpoint, package/version, and stderr/stdout that broke.

tasks.title: Harden registry truth edges and align the named-install/scoped-key contract
tasks.description:
The live verifier should not be able to report green while registry state is half-committed or the public docs lie about dependency declaration. Tighten publish/download ordering so blob truth exists before metadata/counters advance, correct `meshpkg install <name>` messaging to match its real fetch+lockfile behavior, and update the public tooling docs so scoped dependency examples use quoted TOML keys and the operator contract matches what the CLI actually does.

## Failure Modes

| Dependency | On error | On timeout | On malformed response |
|------------|----------|-----------|----------------------|
| Registry DB insert / R2 upload | Abort publish without leaving a metadata-only success path; surface 409 vs internal errors distinctly. | Fail the publish phase and record whether blob upload or DB persistence stalled. | Return an explicit failure instead of claiming success or leaving counters/metadata advanced. |
| Registry object fetch / counter update | Return 404/explicit failure without incrementing counters when the blob is unavailable. | Fail the download phase without mutating counters. | Treat missing blob/metadata state as a release-blocking proof failure. |
| CLI/docs consumers | Fail contract checks when output/docs still imply manifest mutation or omit quoted scoped dependency examples. | N/A | Treat stale contract text as drift to fix, not as a non-blocking note. |

## Load Profile

- **Shared resources**: versions table uniqueness, R2 object existence/lookups, registry download counters, and the live proof package namespace.
- **Per-operation cost**: one blob existence/upload decision plus one DB insert per publish, and one object fetch plus counter update per successful download.
- **10x breakpoint**: duplicate publish races and repeated object lookups would surface first, so the hardened path should stay idempotent and keep the verifier serialized to one version per run.

## Negative Tests

- **Malformed inputs**: duplicate name/version publishes, missing object state, and scoped dependency snippets written without quoted keys.
- **Error paths**: duplicate publish must stay a 409, missing blob fetch must fail without counter inflation, and named install must leave `mesh.toml` unchanged while still updating `mesh.lock`.
- **Boundary conditions**: existing blob reuse by SHA, exact latest-version metadata, and docs/CLI wording that distinguishes declared dependencies from one-off fetches.

## Steps

1. Reorder `registry/src/routes/publish.rs` so object truth is established before the version row becomes the source of public metadata truth, while keeping README extraction and immutable-version handling intact.
2. Reorder `registry/src/routes/download.rs` so counters move only after object fetch succeeds, and make the failure path visible instead of silently inflating download counts.
3. Fix `compiler/meshpkg/src/install.rs` messaging/comments so named install is explicitly a fetch+lockfile operation, not a hidden `mesh.toml` mutation.
4. Update `website/docs/docs/tooling/index.md` and extend `scripts/verify-m034-s01.sh` so the public/operator contract now covers quoted scoped dependency keys, named-install manifest stability, duplicate publish rejection, and the hardened failure expectations.

## Must-Haves

- [ ] Publish no longer leaves a version row as the public truth source before the blob exists.
- [ ] Download counters advance only after object fetch succeeds.
- [ ] CLI/docs explicitly state that named install fetches and locks a package but does not edit `mesh.toml`.
- [ ] The verifier reruns the duplicate-publish and named-install contract checks against the hardened flow.
tasks.verify:
`bash scripts/verify-m034-s01.sh`
`rg -n '"your-login/your-package" = "1.0.0"' website/docs/docs/tooling/index.md`
`rg -n 'does not edit mesh.toml|updates mesh.lock' website/docs/docs/tooling/index.md compiler/meshpkg/src/install.rs`
tasks.observability_impact:
- Signals added/changed: publish/download failures remain visible as 409/404 or explicit verifier phase failures instead of false-green metadata/counter state.
- How a future agent inspects this: rerun `bash scripts/verify-m034-s01.sh` and inspect the publish/download phase logs under `.tmp/m034-s01/verify/`.
- Failure state exposed: whether the break came from blob upload ordering, download object lookup, or stale named-install/scoped-key contract text.

decisions.decision: First serious proof bar
decisions.choice: Plan for a real on-chain creator→fund→claim→treasury loop, not a mostly mocked or simulated demo
decisions.rationale: This materially changes the milestone shape and is central to the product and hackathon story

decisions.decision: Treasury architecture
decisions.choice: Architect the treasury around both buyback-and-burn and dividend-style routing, while sequencing live proof rather than trying to perfect both at once
decisions.rationale: The user wants both modes supported, but the first live milestone still needs a focused path
decisions.revisable: Yes — if execution evidence shows one mode should become canonical

decisions.decision: Primary planning focus for the repo during milestone bootstrap
decisions.choice: Anchor the GSD project contract around Mesh language maturity rather than the creator-fund product
decisions.rationale: The user explicitly chose the language as the primary contract for the new milestone, and M028 is meant to plan the language/platform direction rather than the current product effort.
decisions.revisable: Yes — if the repo returns to product-first planning later

decisions.decision: Primary planning focus for the repo during milestone bootstrap
decisions.choice: Anchor the GSD project contract around Mesh language maturity rather than the creator-fund product
decisions.rationale: The user explicitly chose the language as the primary contract for the new milestone, and M028 is meant to plan the language/platform direction rather than the current product effort.
decisions.revisable: Yes — if the repo returns to product-first planning later

decisions.decision: First serious production proof shape for Mesh
decisions.choice: Use API + DB + migrations + background jobs as the canonical backend path
decisions.rationale: The user wants trust for a real production backend in any capacity, and this backend shape is the first deep proof target before expanding to broader backend forms.
decisions.revisable: Yes — if execution proves a narrower or broader proof path is necessary

decisions.decision: What Mesh should try to beat Elixir on first
decisions.choice: Prioritize easier deployment, raw performance, and better DX before trying to match ecosystem breadth
decisions.rationale: The user defined the early comparison target as easier deployment, raw performance, and better DX, and explicitly called out untrustworthy concurrency and weak docs/examples as unacceptable.
decisions.revisable: Yes — once the trust baseline is established

decisions.decision: How M028 should spend its first milestone energy
decisions.choice: Use evidence-first hardening and honest proof before broad new syntax or feature expansion
decisions.rationale: The repo already exposes a wide backend/runtime/tooling surface; the highest-leverage next step is converting claims into trusted proof rather than increasing surface area without raising credibility.
decisions.revisable: Yes — if the canonical backend path exposes a blocking feature gap that must be added immediately

decisions.decision: Where the canonical backend golden path should live
decisions.choice: Create a new top-level `reference-backend/` Mesh package with env-driven `DATABASE_URL`/`PORT`/`JOB_POLL_MS`, a Postgres `jobs` schema, HTTP create/read endpoints, and a timer-driven worker instead of promoting `mesher/` directly
decisions.rationale: `mesher/` is already a broad product app with clustering, WS, alerting, and frontend concerns. A new narrow package gives S01-S06 one auditable proof target that matches the milestone contract and reuses Mesher only as a donor for patterns.
decisions.revisable: Yes — if execution proves the package location or runtime contract needs to change

decisions.decision: How `reference-backend` HTTP handlers should access the shared Postgres pool
decisions.choice: Add a tiny package-local `RuntimeRegistry` service and have job handlers resolve the pool from it
decisions.rationale: This keeps the reference backend self-contained, avoids importing Mesher’s broader pipeline registry, and preserves the env-driven startup contract without reintroducing inline connection strings in handlers.
decisions.revisable: Yes — if Mesh later gains a cleaner package-local dependency injection pattern

decisions.decision: How Mesh runtime sum/option payloads should carry opaque handles and primitive parse results when compiled Mesh code unwraps them
decisions.choice: Box opaque handles and primitive parsed values before storing them in runtime `MeshResult`/`Option` payloads, then unwrap/dereference them consistently in consumers
decisions.rationale: S01 proved that compiled Mesh programs were crashing because `PoolHandle` and `String.to_int`/`String.to_float` payloads were being stored as raw integer-as-pointer values while compiled Mesh pattern matching and result unwrapping treated them as boxed payload pointers. Boxing the payloads in the runtime fixed migration execution, worker processing, and the reference-backend golden path without leaving an application-level workaround.

decisions.decision: Where the reference-backend startup env validation should happen on the non-empty DATABASE_URL path
decisions.choice: Keep DATABASE_URL/PORT/JOB_POLL_MS validation local to reference-backend/main.mpl using Env.get and Env.get_int before pool startup, instead of routing parsed startup values through imported config helpers
decisions.rationale: S01 reproduced a crash on the real Postgres-backed startup path when the startup parsing/wiring crossed module boundaries in the wrong way. Validating the required env vars locally in main.mpl preserved the explicit missing-env failure mode, let the backend reach /health on a real DATABASE_URL, and gave the runtime-start regression test one stable code path to prove.

decisions.decision: Which surfaces are authoritative for multi-instance exact-once proof on reference-backend
decisions.choice: Use `/health.failed_jobs` and `/health.last_error` as the stable shared-DB contention signal, but prove exact-once and cross-instance participation through direct `jobs` table reads, cross-instance `/jobs/:id`, and per-instance `Job worker processed id=` logs.
decisions.rationale: Under heavier two-instance polling, `/health.processed_jobs` and `/health.last_job_id` can drift enough to cause false negatives even when DB truth is correct. S02 needed a trustworthy proof surface, so exact-once moved to the shared durable row plus HTTP and log cross-checks while `/health` stayed focused on benign-vs-real failure visibility.

decisions.decision: Which surfaces are authoritative for multi-instance exact-once proof on reference-backend
decisions.choice: Use `/health.failed_jobs` and `/health.last_error` as the stable shared-DB contention signal, but prove exact-once and cross-instance participation through direct `jobs` table reads, cross-instance `/jobs/:id`, and per-instance `Job worker processed id=` logs.
decisions.rationale: Under heavier two-instance polling, `/health.processed_jobs` and `/health.last_job_id` can drift enough to cause false negatives even when DB truth is correct. S02 needed a trustworthy proof surface, so exact-once moved to the shared durable row plus HTTP and log cross-checks while `/health` stayed focused on benign-vs-real failure visibility.

decisions.decision: How reference-backend should claim pending jobs under shared Postgres contention
decisions.choice: Keep the fix in application code by implementing claim-next as one `Repo.query_raw(...)` SQL statement with `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` and `RETURNING`, instead of changing `compiler/mesh-rt/src/db/repo.rs`.
decisions.rationale: S02 reproduced ordinary multi-instance claim races inflating worker failure counters. A single claim-and-return SQL statement removes the read/update race window on the real app path without widening runtime surface area, and the existing raw-query seam was already sufficient.

decisions.decision: How S03 should sequence and scope daily-driver tooling trust work
decisions.choice: Execute S03 in this order: formatter/LSP-formatting hardening, then truthful `meshc test` project-dir support plus a real `reference-backend/tests/` path and an honest coverage contract, then JSON-RPC LSP integration proof, then docs/editor truth sync. Do not spend S03 on full line-level coverage instrumentation unless a small real artifact falls out cheaply.
decisions.rationale: The live trust breakers are the formatter panic on `reference-backend`, the false `meshc test` directory workflow, and the green `--coverage` stub. Fixing those first gives the LSP and docs work a verified backend contract instead of speculative behavior. Full coverage instrumentation is deeper than the rest of S03 and would risk delaying the higher-leverage trust repairs.

decisions.decision: How `meshc lsp` should analyze backend-shaped files opened from a real project
decisions.choice: Resolve the nearest ancestor containing `main.mpl` as the project root, analyze dependencies in project order, and overlay open-document contents when producing diagnostics/navigation/formatting results.
decisions.rationale: Single-file analysis was too shallow for `reference-backend/` and produced misleading import failures or stale results. Project-aware resolution plus live-buffer overlays makes editor behavior match the real compiler path and supports trustworthy diagnostics, hover, definition, formatting, and signature help on backend code.

decisions.decision: What `meshc test <path>` should mean on backend-shaped projects
decisions.choice: Use the requested path as the test discovery root, but resolve project import-copy scope from the nearest ancestor containing `main.mpl`; keep `--coverage` as an explicit unsupported non-zero contract until real reporting exists.
decisions.rationale: S03 needed project-root, tests-directory, and specific-file invocation to work on `reference-backend/` without breaking local imports. Splitting discovery scope from project scope preserves the real backend workflow, and failing `--coverage` honestly avoids green placeholder behavior that would undermine tooling trust.

decisions.decision: What boring native deployment path S04 should prove for `reference-backend`
decisions.choice: Use an artifact-first deployment path: stage the compiled `reference-backend` binary plus a deploy-time SQL artifact and thin package-local scripts for staging, SQL apply, and probe-only smoke; keep `meshc migrate` as the developer/build-stage migration flow rather than a runtime-host dependency.
decisions.rationale: Research proved the compiled app binary already runs correctly outside the repo root, while `meshc migrate` remains a compile-at-apply workflow that requires the compiler toolchain, `libmesh_rt.a`, and source-tree context. The lowest-risk honest deployment story is therefore to ship a staged native binary and a boring Postgres migration artifact that can be applied without turning the runtime host into a Mesh compiler workstation.

decisions.decision: How the boring runtime-host schema apply path should relate to the canonical Mesh migration workflow for `reference-backend`
decisions.choice: Keep `reference-backend/migrations/20260323010000_create_jobs.mpl` canonical for compiler-driven dev/CI workflows, but ship a separate checked-in deploy SQL artifact (`reference-backend/deploy/reference-backend.up.sql`) that preserves `_mesh_migrations` and can be applied through `psql` on the runtime host.
decisions.rationale: S04 needed a boring native deployment path that does not require `meshc`, source checkout, or compiler/runtime build assets on the runtime side. Separating the deploy-time SQL artifact from the canonical Mesh migration keeps developer workflows honest while giving operators a thin artifact-first apply path.

decisions.decision: How S05 should sequence supervision and recovery hardening
decisions.choice: Repair and strengthen the Mesh source-level supervisor proof first, then wire `reference-backend` onto that supervisor path, then add abandoned-job recovery plus health visibility, and keep the authoritative end-to-end proof in `compiler/meshc/tests/e2e_reference_backend.rs` with package-local documentation in `reference-backend/README.md`.
decisions.rationale: Research showed the Rust runtime supervisor core is materially stronger than the current Mesh source-level proof, and the compiler/runtime bridge may still be partially broken for real child startup. Building the backend recovery story before that bridge is trustworthy would risk a fake supervision claim. Sequencing the slice this way keeps one authoritative backend harness, closes the real crash-after-claim trust gap, and gives S06 concrete supervision/recovery proof to promote instead of inventing a separate docs-only story.

decisions.decision: How S06 should publish the production backend proof surface
decisions.choice: Gate public promotion on green `reference-backend` proof commands, keep `reference-backend/README.md` as the deepest runbook, add one canonical website proof page, and use targeted cross-links plus a verifier script instead of duplicating long command blocks across generic docs.
decisions.rationale: Research showed the current public docs are toy-first while the real backend proof path had drifted red. Restoring the backend truth gate first and then concentrating public guidance into one canonical narrative keeps R008/R009 honest, reduces maintenance drift, and gives future agents one mechanical doc-truth surface to verify.

decisions.decision: How S07 should close the unfinished recovery contract
decisions.choice: Sequence S07 as: real supervisor-owned restart semantics plus stale/lease reclaim first, then health/restart visibility hardening, then deterministic whole-process restart proof in `compiler/meshc/tests/e2e_reference_backend.rs`; keep doc reconciliation out of S07 and update both the canonical migration and staged deploy SQL artifact if recovery SQL changes.
decisions.rationale: Research showed the live failures come from fake in-worker reboot logic and unsafe blanket reclaim, not from missing documentation. This order fixes the root cause first, preserves S04’s artifact-first deployment contract, and keeps all authoritative recovery proof in the single backend harness that later docs can safely cite.

decisions.decision: How reference-backend should enforce worker restart for the S07 recovery proofs
decisions.choice: Use an explicit watchdog actor that respawns `supervised_job_worker` when `/health` tick-age shows a stuck `processing`/`crashing` worker, instead of relying on the Mesh source-level `supervisor` block for this slice.
decisions.rationale: Live S07 repros showed the worker could enter `crashing` and stop ticking without any real child restart, even though the health contract and tests expected a restarted worker boundary. The watchdog approach stays inside the app proof surface, turns restart ownership into observable behavior immediately, and keeps crash/process-restart proofs green while preserving the existing stale-cutoff reclaim and health metadata contract.

decisions.decision: How S08 should sequence final proof-surface reconciliation
decisions.choice: Execute S08 in three passes: first reconcile the public runbook/proof verifier, then rewrite stale S05/S06 closure artifacts from the green S07 command set, then rerun the full proof surface before sealing milestone validation and R008.
decisions.rationale: Research shows the remaining gap is truth-surface drift, not backend correctness. Fixing the public proof hierarchy first creates a durable guard against doc drift, updating the internal closure artifacts removes contradictory evidence, and delaying milestone/requirement closure until after full reruns keeps the slice evidence-first instead of papering over regressions.

decisions.decision: How the reconciled production proof surface should detect public doc drift
decisions.choice: Use exact string checks in `reference-backend/scripts/verify-production-proof-surface.sh` over the shared recovery-aware command list and `/health` recovery vocabulary across the README and proof page.
decisions.rationale: S08's remaining risk was truth-surface drift, not missing runtime coverage. Exact command and vocabulary checks turn drift into a mechanical verifier failure instead of a manual-reading problem and keep the promoted README/docs surfaces aligned with the green S07 proof contract.

decisions.decision: Which acceptance story should closure artifacts use once the recovery contract is green
decisions.choice: Treat the S07 recovery-aware UAT command order as the canonical acceptance script for reconciled S05, S06, and S08 closure artifacts instead of preserving slice-local historical scripts.
decisions.rationale: Keeping multiple partially overlapping acceptance scripts would preserve contradictory evidence even after S07 closed the runtime gap. Reusing one canonical command order keeps public docs, internal closure artifacts, milestone validation, and requirement tracking on the same proof path.

decisions.decision: How to disambiguate trailing closures from control-flow block openers
decisions.choice: Use parser context: suppress trailing-closure parsing when inside a control-flow condition position (if, while, case, for conditions). The `do` after `)` in those contexts is the block opener, not a trailing closure.
decisions.rationale: The trailing-closure heuristic (`do` after `)` = trailing closure) causes `if fn_call() do` to be misparsed. Context-based disambiguation preserves existing trailing-closure usage (`test("name") do ... end`, `describe("name") do ... end`) while fixing control flow. The alternative (requiring `do \

decisions.decision: Whether bare expression statements (without `let _ =`) should be the idiomatic pattern for side effects in Mesh
decisions.choice: Remove all `let _ =` bindings for side-effectful expressions in both reference-backend and mesher. Bare expression statements are confirmed to work at the codegen level (lower_block handles them correctly).
decisions.rationale: Investigation confirmed that `lower_block` in `compiler/mesh-codegen/src/mir/lower.rs` already handles bare expressions in blocks. Multiple test programs (bare `println()`, `ServiceCall.inc()`, `spawn()`) compiled and ran correctly without `let _ =`. The 157+72 instances in dogfood code are unnecessary ceremony.

decisions.decision: When to replace `<>` string concatenation with `#{}` interpolation in dogfood cleanup
decisions.choice: Only replace `<>` with interpolation where the result is clearly more readable. Keep `<>` for raw SQL construction with complex expressions and raw JSONB embedding where interpolation would be awkward.
decisions.rationale: Not all `<>` usage is bad. Cases like `"too many events (max " <> String.from(n) <> ")"` are clearly better as `"too many events (max #{n})"`. But SQL construction like `"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS events_" <> date_str <> " PARTITION OF events..."` is borderline — interpolation works but the `<>` makes the SQL template structure more visible. Apply judgment per-case.

decisions.decision: How to fix else-if chain type resolution in the typechecker
decisions.choice: Add `types.insert(if_.syntax().text_range(), resolved_type)` before both return paths in `infer_if`, matching the storage pattern `infer_expr` uses for all other expression types.
decisions.rationale: The root cause was `infer_if` bypassing `infer_expr` during recursion, so the inner if-expression's resolved type was never stored in the `types` map. Codegen fell back to `MirType::Unit`, producing garbage values (Int) or misaligned pointer crashes (String). The fix is the minimal insertion that makes recursive inference consistent with the top-level inference path.

decisions.decision: Where to handle parenthesized import formatting in the formatter walker
decisions.choice: Extract a dedicated `walk_import_list` function that handles the `IMPORT_LIST` node, rather than modifying `walk_from_import_decl`, because `L_PAREN`/`R_PAREN` tokens live inside `IMPORT_LIST` in the CST, not as direct children of `FROM_IMPORT_DECL`.
decisions.rationale: The plan assumed parens would be at the `FROM_IMPORT_DECL` level, but CST inspection showed they're children of `IMPORT_LIST`. Handling them at the correct tree level avoids fragile parent-child assumptions and keeps the walker dispatch consistent with CST structure.

decisions.decision: Whether to convert mesher long import lines to parenthesized multiline form
decisions.choice: Deferred — meshc fmt collapses parenthesized multiline imports back to single-line and introduces spurious spaces in module dot-paths (e.g. `Storage. Schema`), making `fmt --check` fail. Parser supports the syntax correctly; the issue is purely in the formatter walker.
decisions.rationale: The parser proven by e2e tests handles multiline imports correctly, but the formatter undoes them. Applying multiline imports only to have `fmt --check` permanently fail is worse than keeping long single-line imports until the formatter is fixed.

decisions.decision: Whether `do...end` blocks can be used as values in `let` bindings
decisions.choice: No — `let x = do ... end` is not valid Mesh syntax. Block-as-expression context is provided by if/else, case, and function bodies, not standalone `do...end`.
decisions.rationale: Discovered during S05 test authoring: the parser does not accept `do...end` as a value expression in let bindings. This is consistent with how Mesh structures block contexts through control flow and function bodies rather than arbitrary block expressions.

decisions.decision: How to handle mesher JSON serializers with raw JSONB fields
decisions.choice: Use `json {}` macro where all fields are simple strings/ints; use `#{}` interpolation where raw JSONB fields (condition_json, action_json, tags, exception, stacktrace, breadcrumbs, extra, user_context, condition_snapshot) prevent macro use
decisions.rationale: The json macro auto-quotes string values, which would double-quote already-JSON JSONB fields. Interpolation gives explicit control over quoting. This splits the work cleanly: simple serializers get the macro, complex ones get interpolation — both are better than 300-char `<>` chains.

decisions.decision: When to satisfy the reference-backend dotted-path repair requirement in M029
decisions.choice: Complete the `reference-backend/` import repair in S01 alongside the formatter fix, instead of deferring it to S03.
decisions.rationale: R027 is a direct S01 requirement in the slice brief, and `meshc fmt --check reference-backend` can pass on already-corrupted but idempotent source. S01 therefore has to leave the dogfood backend in a semantically correct round-trippable state, not just ship formatter unit fixes.

decisions.decision: How mesh-fmt should emit CST PATH nodes used by imports and qualified impl headers
decisions.choice: Route `SyntaxKind::PATH` through a dedicated `walk_path(...)` formatter that preserves dot-separated token text instead of using the generic inline spacing walker.
decisions.rationale: Imports (`from Api.Router import ...`) and qualified impl headers (`impl Foo.Bar for Baz.Qux do`) both reuse the parser's `PATH` node. The generic inline walker inserts spaces before identifiers after `.` and corrupts semantically meaningful module/type paths into `Foo. Bar`. A dedicated PATH walker localizes the fix to the shared root cause without broad spacing changes elsewhere in the formatter.

decisions.decision: How to prove Mesher pipe cleanup and remaining `<>` sites in M029/S02
decisions.choice: Use `rg -n 'List\.map\(rows,\
decisions.rationale: Ok\(List\.map\(' mesher -g '*.mpl'` as the authoritative pipe-style gate, and prove remaining `<>` usage with an exact-location diff against the five designated SQL/DDL keep sites.
decisions.revisable: The roadmap's broad `rg 'List\.map(' mesher -g '*.mpl'` check would false-fail on the desired pipe style because it matches both wrapping and piped `List.map(...)` calls. The exact-location `<>` diff prevents future work from silently introducing new concatenation sites while still preserving the accepted SQL/DDL assemblies in `schema.mpl` and `queries.mpl`.

decisions.decision: How to decompose M029/S03's Mesher formatter-closeout work
decisions.choice: Split S03 into two manual multiline-import rewrite tasks, three directory-scoped formatter canonicalization waves, and one final proof task that writes `S03-UAT.md`, while keeping `reference-backend/` regression-only unless formatter behavior regresses.
decisions.rationale: Live S03 research reduced the true hand-edit surface to 10 imports across 8 files, but the remaining Mesher formatter-red set still spans 35 files. A single bulk formatter task would be too large and opaque for one executor window, while splitting by real source clusters keeps each task reviewable, preserves explicit dependencies from manual import edits into formatter waves, and leaves the final task responsible for the full dual-app proof surface plus a durable closeout artifact.

decisions.decision: Canonical source-file, pipe-chain, and struct-literal formatting behavior in mesh-fmt
decisions.choice: Top-level formatter output now keeps adjacent comment lines and import runs compact, flattens pipe chains to a single continuation indent, and forces multiline struct-literal closure bodies when needed instead of collapsing them into one line.
decisions.rationale: Mesher cleanup exposed three formatter regressions at once: blank lines between every top-level comment/import, stair-stepped pipe indentation, and inline closure bodies with multi-field struct literals collapsing into unreadable single lines. Fixing all three in the formatter keeps canonical output readable while preserving the existing blank-line rule for real top-level item boundaries.

decisions.decision: How final formatter-closeout gates should treat success output and spacing-sensitive regressions
decisions.choice: Keep `meshc fmt --check` silent on success and fix formatter/CLI regressions with exact-output tests instead of weakening captured-log acceptance gates.
decisions.rationale: S03 closeout exposed two real truth-surface failures: formatter spacing regressions (`pubtype`, `table"..."`) and non-empty output on successful `fmt --check` runs. The slice used captured-log emptiness as part of the acceptance contract, so relaxing that gate would hide regressions instead of fixing them. Silent success plus exact-output tests keeps formatter proof text-sensitive rather than exit-code-only.

decisions.decision: What bar M032 should use for language and tooling changes
decisions.choice: Focus on proven blockers and stale-workaround retirement rather than broad language-design expansion
decisions.rationale: The current milestone is meant to make mesher's limitation surface truthful and retire the blockers that actually affect it now. A wide design sprint would dilute proof and risk replacing folklore with new speculative surface area.
decisions.revisable: Yes — if a broader change is the only honest way to retire a proven blocker

decisions.decision: How to sequence the current mesher dogfood cleanup and data-layer work
decisions.choice: Split the work into M032 for workaround-truth plus Mesh blocker retirement and M033 for ORM expressiveness plus schema extras
decisions.rationale: The current pressure actually has two different jobs: first remove stale folklore and fix still-real Mesh blockers, then improve the ORM and migration surfaces against a cleaner truthful mesher baseline. Combining them would blur root-cause fixing with API expansion and make proof weaker.
decisions.revisable: Yes — if M032 discovers substantially more ORM-only pressure than expected

decisions.decision: How the ORM and migration surfaces should balance portability with database-specific behavior
decisions.choice: Keep a neutral core and add explicit vendor-specific extras instead of pretending all recurring mesher pressure is portable
decisions.rationale: The user wants portability without fake abstraction. Mesher's real pressure points are not generic SQL; they include JSONB, full-text, pgcrypto, and partition DDL. Making vendor-specific behavior explicit is more honest than hiding it behind raw fragments or a misleading neutral API.
decisions.revisable: Yes — if execution shows one layer belongs in the core after all

decisions.decision: Which vendor-specific data-layer work lands first
decisions.choice: Implement Postgres extras first and defer SQLite extras until the neutral extension shape is proven
decisions.rationale: Mesher is the real proof surface for this wave and it is currently applying Postgres-specific pressure. Shipping PG extras first keeps the work anchored to live demand while still preserving a clean path for SQLite later.
decisions.revisable: Yes — if a near-term SQLite-backed dogfood target appears

decisions.decision: What bar M032 should use for language and tooling changes
decisions.choice: Focus on proven blockers and stale-workaround retirement rather than broad language-design expansion
decisions.rationale: The current milestone is meant to make mesher's limitation surface truthful and retire the blockers that actually affect it now. A wide design sprint would dilute proof and risk replacing folklore with new speculative surface area.
decisions.revisable: Yes — if a broader change is the only honest way to retire a proven blocker

decisions.decision: How much mesher itself is allowed to move during M032 and M033
decisions.choice: Use mesher as the proof target but keep its product behavior stable; improve Mesh and the data layer underneath it instead of redesigning the app
decisions.rationale: The user wants mesher to prove the repaired platform path, not become a moving product target. Stable behavior makes it easier to distinguish real platform gains from app-level rewrites.
decisions.revisable: Yes — if a narrow product-facing change is unavoidable to dogfood the repaired path honestly

decisions.decision: How M032/S01 records limitation truth for mesher folklore
decisions.choice: Keep both permanent Rust e2e tests and a direct fixture replay script; the script must exercise the `.tmp/m032-s01` programs from repo root and use a live HTTP request for route closures instead of relying on compile-only builds.
decisions.rationale: The stale-vs-real audit spans both passing and failing behaviors, and one of the retained limits (`route_closure`) is compile-pass but runtime-fail. Encoding the proofs only in Rust tests would hide the exact audited commands, while compile-only replay would misclassify the HTTP closure limitation. Using both surfaces leaves later slices with durable named tests and a direct matrix replay that exposes the real failure symptoms.

decisions.decision: How S02 will satisfy the mesher dogfood requirement for the inferred-export fix
decisions.choice: Use `mesher/storage/writer.mpl` as the real dogfood surface by exporting the inferred-parameter batch-write helper now living beside `insert_event`, and have `mesher/services/writer.mpl` import it instead of adding a synthetic repro-only helper.
decisions.rationale: R013 requires a real blocker to be fixed in Mesh and then used in mesher. Existing comment cleanup alone is too weak, but inventing a synthetic exported identity helper would fake product pressure. Extracting the batch-write helper keeps product behavior stable, exercises the repaired inferred-export path on a real module boundary, and naturally pairs with the required wording cleanup in `mesher/storage/writer.mpl`.

decisions.decision: How to repair unconstrained inferred function exports without widening Mesh's generic machinery
decisions.choice: Collect concrete function-usage signatures from local and cross-module call sites, pass that evidence into MIR lowering, and emit per-signature MIR clones only when an inferred function is used at multiple concrete ABIs.
decisions.rationale: The `identity(x) = x` blocker was not a general typechecker failure; it was a lowering-time ABI problem. Recovering only the first observed parameter type still left later call sites miscompiled (`i64 -> {}` for local polymorphic uses) and exported modules lowered in isolation never saw downstream evidence at all. A narrow usage-driven specialization path fixes the real blocker, keeps existing already-constrained inferred functions unchanged, and avoids turning S02 into a broad generic-function redesign.

decisions.decision: How M032/S03 should retire stale request/handler folklore in mesher
decisions.choice: Use currently supported inline Mesh patterns directly in the real mesher handlers, while preserving only the verified route-closure, nested-&&, and timer keep-site comments.
decisions.rationale: The stale wrappers and limitation comments in mesher were making Mesh look weaker than current reality. Dogfooding Request.query, inline service-call case handling, and inline cast if/else in the audited modules retires folklore without widening scope or erasing the still-real limits that S01 already proved.

decisions.decision: How S04 should reconcile solved cross-module from_json support with Mesher's event ingestion/storage path
decisions.choice: Retire stale module-boundary from_json comments, but keep Mesher's SQL-side extract/write path unchanged until a separate parity proof justifies a Mesh-side rewrite
decisions.rationale: Cross-module from_json is already supported and regression-covered, but Mesher's fingerprint fallback chain still depends on PostgreSQL JSONB operators plus CASE/jsonb_array_elements/string_agg, and the matching insert path already lives on the same raw-SQL side of the boundary. Replacing that path in S04 would be a product-path redesign without parity evidence, not an honest folklore cleanup.

decisions.decision: How M033 should sequence ORM expressiveness and schema-extra work
decisions.choice: Sequence M033 as neutral write-expression core first, then explicit PostgreSQL JSONB/search/crypto extras, then harder read-side raw-boundary retirement, then partition/schema lifecycle helpers with live catalog proof, and finish with public docs plus integrated Mesher acceptance.
decisions.rationale: The highest risk is getting the neutral-core boundary wrong. Proving computed writes first validates the reusable expression contract without overbuilding. PG-heavy families should land only after that contract is real, the harder read-side families should stop at honest coverage instead of chasing a fake universal AST, partition helpers need their own live Postgres proof surface, and docs must come last so they describe shipped behavior rather than anticipated API shape.

decisions.decision: How S01 should represent the neutral write-expression core
decisions.choice: Add a dedicated neutral expression builder plus expression-aware Query/Repo entrypoints, and keep PG-specific helpers out of that core.
decisions.rationale: The current Query/Repo surface already uses opaque builder pointers. Extending that model with a dedicated neutral expression layer is the cleanest way to cover column refs, literals, NULL, function calls, CASE, COALESCE, and conflict-update expressions without smuggling PostgreSQL syntax through raw strings. This keeps R036 honest, preserves the later SQLite extension seam required by R040, and lets Mesher rewrite portable write paths now while leaving JSONB/crypto/search-specific helpers explicit for later slices.

decisions.decision: How S02 should expose PostgreSQL-only JSONB/search/pgcrypto behavior without reopening the neutral core
decisions.choice: Add PG-only expression helpers under `Pg`, extend the existing expression/query/repo plumbing just enough for casts, expression-valued SELECT/WHERE, and expression-valued inserts, and keep `extract_event_fields` as an explicit raw keep-site unless the helper path stays obviously honest.
decisions.rationale: S01 already established a neutral expression core and reserved query plumbing (`select_params`) that explicit PG helpers can compose with. Keeping vendor-specific behavior under `Pg` advances R037 while preserving the R040 SQLite seam. Deferring `extract_event_fields` unless it stays clearly honest avoids overbuilding a fake universal read DSL and keeps the raw boundary truthful for R038/S03.

decisions.decision: How Mesher rate-limit thresholds are configured for live ingest
decisions.choice: Keep the 60-second / 1000-event defaults, but read optional MESHER_RATE_LIMIT_WINDOW_SECONDS and MESHER_RATE_LIMIT_MAX_EVENTS env vars and spawn the window-reset ticker from the rate-limiter startup helper.
decisions.rationale: This preserves existing production behavior while making the live ingest limiter truthfully testable at small thresholds and restoring the intended fixed-window reset behavior.

decisions.decision: What Mesh-callable aliasing surface S01 should use for expression-valued SELECT items until the parser keyword collision is repaired
decisions.choice: Use `Expr.label(...)` as the public Mesh-callable alias entrypoint for now, while still lowering to the same runtime alias intrinsic as `mesh_expr_alias`.
decisions.rationale: `Expr.alias(...)` currently collides with keyword parsing after `Expr.` in Mesh source. S01 needed a working SELECT aliasing surface immediately to prove the neutral expression contract, and `Expr.label(...)` preserves that capability without widening the slice into parser surgery.

decisions.decision: What Mesh-callable aliasing surface S01 should use for expression-valued SELECT items until the parser keyword collision is repaired
decisions.choice: Use `Expr.label(...)` as the public Mesh-callable alias entrypoint for now, while still lowering to the same runtime alias intrinsic as `mesh_expr_alias`.
decisions.rationale: `Expr.alias(...)` currently collides with keyword parsing after `Expr.` in Mesh source. S01 needed a working SELECT aliasing surface immediately to prove the neutral expression contract, and `Expr.label(...)` preserves that capability without widening the slice into parser surgery.

decisions.decision: How the async StorageWriter path should carry identity and flush low-volume event batches on the live Mesher ingest path
decisions.choice: Buffered writer entries now carry `project_id|||issue_id|||fingerprint|||event_json`, writers are started through `start_writer(...)` so the timer flush ticker is always spawned, and writer flush helpers return non-integer success markers when the count is not needed.
decisions.rationale: The live S01 proof showed that low-volume repeated ingest updated `issues` but did not persist `events` because the flush ticker was never started, the writer state's `project_id` label was not the UUID needed by the raw event insert, and `Int ! String` success payloads were still brittle enough to crash the background path. Encoding the real project UUID into the buffered payload and using the timer-backed start helper makes the persistence boundary truthful for downstream slices.

decisions.decision: How S02 JSONB rewrites should express PG-native extract/build/existence operations that do not yet have dedicated Pg wrappers
decisions.choice: Use Expr.fn_call(...) with PostgreSQL-native functions like jsonb_extract_path, jsonb_extract_path_text, jsonb_exists, and jsonb_build_object alongside the explicit Pg casts/operators, instead of expanding the dedicated Pg helper surface further inside S02.
decisions.rationale: The T01/T02 expression seam already supports structured, parameterized vendor-specific function calls without pretending those operations are portable. Reusing Expr.fn_call keeps Mesher on Query.where_expr / Query.select_expr / Repo.insert_expr for the real runtime path, shrinks raw whole-query usage, and avoids inventing a wider fake-neutral AST or rushing new one-off Pg wrappers before there is broader demand.

decisions.decision: How the S02 PG-helper acceptance suite should exercise real Mesher storage paths
decisions.choice: Use temporary Mesh projects that copy Mesher storage/types modules for the S02 Postgres proof bundle instead of routing the slice verification through the S01 HTTP readiness harness.
decisions.rationale: This lets the proof bundle call the real auth/search/jsonb/alert storage helpers directly against live Postgres while avoiding unrelated HTTP startup/readiness noise, so failures localize to the new PG helper boundary instead of to Mesher server boot behavior.

decisions.decision: How S03 should retire the hard read-side raw families without reopening the neutral ORM boundary
decisions.choice: Prefer Mesher-side decomposition plus the existing `Query` / `Expr` / `Pg` surfaces for S03 read rewrites, and keep only an explicit named leftover list instead of adding general derived-table or scalar-subquery support in this slice.
decisions.rationale: The current runtime already covers most projection, filter, join, grouping, and ordering work honestly, and the remaining hard read families can be retired or narrowed through multiple smaller builder-backed reads plus Mesh-side combination. Adding a broader derived-table or scalar-subquery surface here would overstate portability, recreate the fake-universal-AST risk already called out in M033 planning, and work against the R036/R040 requirement to keep the neutral baseline honest while leaving vendor-specific behavior explicit.

decisions.decision: Composed-read verification surface for Mesher S03
decisions.choice: Use the live Mesher HTTP/API harness for composed-read proofs instead of extending the copied storage-only Mesh probe
decisions.rationale: The storage-only probe already reproduced compiler/runtime artifacts that are specific to staged typed-list and aggregate consumption rather than the actual Mesher caller contracts. Replacing it with a live Mesher-backed harness keeps verification aligned with the real search/dashboard/detail/alerts/team surfaces and exposes genuine contract regressions separately from probe-only failures.

decisions.decision: How live Mesher event-alert cooldown eligibility should be determined
decisions.choice: Filter cooldown-eligible event rules inside `get_event_alert_rules(...)` and treat the selector as the fireable-rule boundary instead of doing a second per-rule cooldown read in the alert loop.
decisions.rationale: The live `/api/v1/events` alert path became unstable when cooldown was checked in a separate follow-up read. Folding cooldown into the selector kept the data contract on one explicit SQL boundary and restored deterministic alert firing in the S03 acceptance harness.

decisions.decision: How S03 should treat read helpers whose builder-backed live path still mis-shapes rows or crashes Mesher
decisions.choice: Keep those read families as explicit named raw keep-sites in `mesher/storage/queries.mpl` and enforce that boundary with `scripts/verify-m033-s03.sh` instead of forcing a dishonest builder rewrite.
decisions.rationale: The live Postgres-backed S03 harness showed that some read families were still unstable on the real Mesher caller path when forced through the current builder/runtime combination. Preserving them as named keep-sites keeps the raw boundary honest, prevents fake portability claims, and gives S04/S05 a mechanically enforced ledger of what still remains outside the current honest ORM surface.

decisions.decision: What the neutral migration index surface should cover in M033/S04
decisions.choice: Keep `Migration.create_index(...)` strictly neutral: support exact `name:...`, `unique:true|false`, trailing `where:...`, and per-column `:ASC` / `:DESC`, while rejecting PostgreSQL-specific method/opclass syntax and routing those cases through explicit `Pg.*` helpers.
decisions.rationale: Mesher needed stable index names, partial predicates, and ordered columns on the honest portable path, but pretending `USING`, opclasses, or partition DDL are neutral would blur the vendor boundary and recreate the fake-portability problem M033 is trying to remove.

decisions.decision: Where Mesher should own runtime partition lifecycle after the S04 schema-helper work
decisions.choice: Keep Mesher's runtime partition create/list/drop lifecycle in `Storage.Schema` as thin wrappers over explicit `Pg.create_daily_partitions_ahead(...)`, `Pg.list_daily_partitions_before(...)`, and `Pg.drop_partition(...)`, and remove those helpers from `Storage.Queries` instead of widening the generic query API.
decisions.rationale: Partition bootstrap, retention cleanup, and catalog-oriented drop behavior are explicitly PostgreSQL-shaped schema concerns. Centralizing them in `Storage.Schema` keeps the query layer focused on row access, preserves an honest boundary for future vendor-specific extras, and gives startup/retention code one authoritative helper surface.

decisions.decision: How S04 should enforce the migration/runtime partition helper boundary after the live Postgres proof lands
decisions.choice: Treat `compiler/meshc/tests/e2e_m033_s04.rs` plus `scripts/verify-m033-s04.sh` as the authoritative S04 acceptance surface, and remove the old S03 exemption for partition/catalog helpers so raw-boundary regressions fail mechanically in the named migration/runtime files.
decisions.rationale: The slice promise is not just helper availability but a durable honest boundary. A live catalog harness plus a verifier that bans raw DDL/query tokens and checks expected helper/log surfaces turns future drift into named failures instead of folklore or silent regressions.

decisions.decision: Canonical S05 acceptance surface and documentation truth gate
decisions.choice: Close S05 with a public docs rewrite plus `scripts/verify-m033-s05.sh`, a serial wrapper that runs the existing S02/S03/S04 verifiers, VitePress docs build, and an exact-string docs-truth sweep instead of starting with a new `e2e_m033_s05.rs` Rust harness.
decisions.rationale: S02-S04 already provide the live Postgres runtime and catalog proof surfaces that S05 needs to compose. The remaining gap is milestone-level replay and public contract honesty, not another runtime subsystem. A serial wrapper preserves the shared Postgres/port-5432 assumption, keeps failures localized to the first broken phase, and makes the remaining raw boundary plus neutral-vs-PG split mechanically enforceable in public docs without duplicating harness boilerplate.

decisions.decision: Which slice owns `get_event_alert_rules(...)` and `get_threshold_rules(...)` in the M033 raw-boundary ledger
decisions.choice: Treat both functions as deliberate S03 honest raw-read keep-sites, not S02 PG-helper-owned builder coverage.
decisions.rationale: The assembled S05 acceptance surface and public docs need one truthful owner for the remaining raw read families. These selectors now belong to the S03 named raw keep-list; keeping them there aligns the verifiers, the public contract, and the real Mesher read-side/runtime behavior instead of letting S02 claim builder coverage it no longer owns.

decisions.decision: Where the package-manager UX improvement wave should focus
decisions.choice: Center package-manager experience improvements on the public packages website first, with CLI polish secondary, and do that after the underlying trust path is proven.
decisions.rationale: The user said the package-manager UI is simple and chose website-first when asked where the improvement effort should center. Sequencing it after M034 keeps polish from outrunning truth.
decisions.revisable: Yes — if later discussion or execution shows the biggest usability pain is actually CLI-first.

decisions.decision: How to sequence the next Mesh maturity wave after M033
decisions.choice: Split the next maturity wave into four milestones sequenced as M034 delivery truth, M035 test-framework hardening, M036 editor parity and multi-editor support, and M037 package experience polish.
decisions.rationale: The user’s vision spans CI/CD hardening, package-manager end-to-end proof, test-framework readiness, editor support, and package UI improvement. Splitting these into four milestones preserves the full ambition without collapsing unlike work into one mushy milestone.
decisions.revisable: Yes — if execution evidence shows one milestone should merge or split further.

decisions.decision: How serious multi-editor support should be
decisions.choice: Treat non-VSCode editor support as a first-class milestone goal rather than a best-effort secondary story.
decisions.rationale: The user explicitly rejected VS Code-only and best-effort secondary support, and wants a real path for Vim or another non-VSCode editor.
decisions.revisable: Yes — once the first serious secondary editor target is chosen during M036 discussion.

decisions.decision: Where the package-manager UX improvement wave should focus
decisions.choice: Center package-manager experience improvements on the public packages website first, with CLI polish secondary, and do that after the underlying trust path is proven.
decisions.rationale: The user said the package-manager UI is simple and chose website-first when asked where the improvement effort should center. Sequencing it after M034 keeps polish from outrunning truth.
decisions.revisable: Yes — if later discussion or execution shows the biggest usability pain is actually CLI-first.

decisions.decision: What should be the canonical proof surface for the real registry publish/install path
decisions.choice: Use a repo-local `scripts/verify-m034-s01.sh` verifier with one owner-scoped proof package name, unique per-run versions, `meshpkg --json`, direct registry HTTP checks, lockfile inspection, consumer build proof, duplicate publish rejection, and exact package-page visibility checks.
decisions.rationale: This creates one observable proof surface that later CI slices can call unchanged and avoids treating artifact builds, homepage counts, or undeclared assumptions as release confidence.

decisions.decision: What should be the canonical proof surface for the real registry publish/install path
decisions.choice: Use a repo-local `scripts/verify-m034-s01.sh` verifier with one owner-scoped proof package name, unique per-run versions, `meshpkg --json`, direct registry HTTP checks, lockfile inspection, consumer build proof, duplicate publish rejection, and exact package-page visibility checks.
decisions.rationale: This creates one observable proof surface that later CI slices can call unchanged and avoids treating artifact builds, homepage counts, or undeclared assumptions as release confidence.

decisions.decision: How scoped installed registry packages should be represented and discovered locally
decisions.choice: Keep the current owner/package@version on-disk layout under `.mesh/packages` and fix `meshc`/`mesh-lsp` discovery to recurse to leaf directories containing `mesh.toml`, rather than sanitizing scoped package names into a flat cache key.
decisions.rationale: This removes the real scoped-install build blocker without inventing a second name-encoding contract, preserves the registry/package name end-to-end, and keeps compiler and editor behavior aligned on the same package-root rule.

verification_evidence.command: cargo build -p mesh-rt && cargo run -q -p meshc -- build mesher && cargo run -q -p meshc -- fmt --check mesher
python3 raw-boundary scan + rg extract_event_fields keep-site

rg -n "Expr\\.label|Expr\\.value|Expr\\.column|Expr\\.null|Expr\\.case_when|Expr\\.coalesce|Query\\.where_expr|Query\\.select_exprs|Repo\\.insert_expr|Repo\\.update_where_expr|Repo\\.insert_or_update_expr|Migration\\.create_index|Repo\\.query_raw|Repo\\.execute_raw|Migration\\.execute|Pg\\.|SQLite-specific extras are later work" website/docs/docs/databases/index.md

test -f .tmp/m033-s05/verify/01-docs-build.log && test -f .tmp/m033-s05/verify/02-docs-truth.log && test -f .tmp/m033-s05/verify/03-verify-m033-s02.log && test -f .tmp/m033-s05/verify/04-verify-m033-s03.log && test -f .tmp/m033-s05/verify/05-verify-m033-s04.log && ls -1 .tmp/m033-s05/verify
